1→# Core Data Model: SouvenirModel
     2→
     3→## ⚠️ CRITICAL: DATA MODEL PROTECTION POLICY ⚠️
     4→
     5→**THIS SCHEMA IS THE CANONICAL SOURCE OF TRUTH FOR THE SOUVENIR DATA MODEL.**
     6→
     7→### Immutability Rules
     8→1. **NEVER delete entities from the Core Data model** (`SouvenirModel.xcdatamodeld`)
     9→2. **NEVER remove attributes or relationships** without a formal migration plan
    10→3. **ALL schema changes require lightweight or heavyweight migration strategy**
    11→4. If an entity or attribute appears to be missing from the `.xcdatamodeld` file in Xcode:
    12→   - **RESTORE IT IMMEDIATELY** using this schema as the reference
    13→   - Compare against the generated `*+CoreDataProperties.swift` files
    14→   - Verify all four entities exist: ContactEntity, PhotoEntity, SouvenirEntity, ShareEntity
    15→5. Before any schema modification, increment the model version and plan migration
    16→
    17→### Model Validation Checklist
    18→Run this checklist before any commit that touches Core Data:
    19→- [ ] All four entities exist: ContactEntity, PhotoEntity, SouvenirEntity, ShareEntity
    20→- [ ] All attributes match this schema (names, types, optionality)
    21→- [ ] All relationships match this schema (cardinality, inverse relationships)
    22→- [ ] Codegen is set to "Manual/None" for all entities
    23→- [ ] Generated Swift files exist and compile: `Models*+CoreDataProperties.swift` and `Models*+CoreDataClass.swift`
    24→- [ ] PersistenceController references "SouvenirModel" correctly
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→**Note:** This file documents the Core Data model schema. You must create `SouvenirModel.xcdatamodeld` in Xcode with these specifications.
    29→
    30→## Entities
    31→
    32→### ContactEntity
    33→
    34→| Attribute            | Type        | Optional | Default | Indexed |
    35→| -------------------- | ----------- | -------- | ------- | ------- |
    36→| id                   | UUID        | No       | -       | Yes     |
    37→| compositeName        | String      | Yes      | -       | No      |
    38→| givenName            | String      | Yes      | -       | Yes     |
    39→| familyName           | String      | Yes      | -       | Yes     |
    40→| phoneE164            | String      | Yes      | -       | Yes     |
    41→| notes                | String      | Yes      | -       | No      |
    42→| createdAt            | Date        | No       | Date()  | No      |
    43→| externalIdentifier   | String      | Yes      | -       | Yes     |
    44→| imageThumbData       | Binary Data | Yes      | -       | No      |
    45→| organization         | String      | Yes      | -       | No      |
    46→| email                | String      | Yes      | -       | No      |
    47→| address              | String      | Yes      | -       | No      |
    48→| birthday             | Date        | Yes      | -       | No      |
    49→| isPrimaryForSouvenir | Boolean     | No       | false   | No      |
    50→
    51→**Relationships:**
    52→
    53→- `photos`: To-Many → PhotoEntity (inverse: `contacts`)
    54→- `primarySouvenir`: To-One → SouvenirEntity (inverse: `primaryContact`)
    55→
    56→---
    57→
    58→### PhotoEntity
    59→
    60→| Attribute           | Type    | Optional | Default | Indexed |
    61→| ------------------- | ------- | -------- | ------- | ------- |
    62→| id                  | UUID    | No       | -       | Yes     |
    63→| accentColorHex      | String  | Yes      | -       | No      |
    64→| assetLocalId        | String  | Yes      | -       | Yes     |
    65→| timestamp           | Date    | Yes      | -       | Yes     |
    66→| timezoneID          | String  | Yes      | -       | No      |
    67→| latitude            | Double  | Yes      | -       | No      |
    68→| longitude           | Double  | Yes      | -       | No      |
    69→| postalGeocodeJSON   | String  | Yes      | -       | No      |
    70→| w3w                 | String  | Yes      | -       | No      |
    71→| plusCode            | String  | Yes      | -       | No      |
    72→| favoriteForSouvenir | Boolean | No       | false   | No      |
    73→| viewportScale       | Double  | No       | 1.0     | No      |
    74→| viewportOffsetX     | Double  | No       | 0.0     | No      |
    75→| viewportOffsetY     | Double  | No       | 0.0     | No      |
    76→| isViewportLocked    | Boolean | No       | false   | No      |
    77→
    78→**Relationships:**
    79→
    80→- `contacts`: To-Many → ContactEntity (inverse: `photos`)
    81→- `souvenirs`: To-Many → SouvenirEntity (inverse: `photos`)
    82→- `favoritedBySouvenir`: To-One → SouvenirEntity (inverse: `favoritePhoto`)
    83→
    84→---
    85→
    86→### SouvenirEntity
    87→
    88→| Attribute            | Type   | Optional | Default | Indexed |
    89→| -------------------- | ------ | -------- | ------- | ------- |
    90→| id                   | UUID   | No       | -       | Yes     |
    91→| accentColorHex       | String | Yes      | -       | No      |
    92→| customAccentColorHex | String | Yes      | -       | No      |
    93→| title                | String | Yes      | -       | No      |
    94→| caption              | String | Yes      | -       | No      |
    95→| createdAt            | Date   | No       | Date()  | No      |
    96→| updatedAt            | Date   | No       | Date()  | Yes     |
    97→| deletedAt            | Date   | Yes      | -       | No      |
    98→| lastLatitude         | Double | Yes      | -       | No      |
    99→| lastLongitude        | Double | Yes      | -       | No      |
   100→| meetPlace            | String | Yes      | -       | No      |
   101→| meetTime             | Date   | Yes      | -       | No      |
   102→
   103→**Relationships:**
   104→
   105→- `primaryContact`: To-One → ContactEntity (inverse: `primarySouvenir`)
   106→- `photos`: To-Many → PhotoEntity (inverse: `souvenirs`)
   107→- `favoritePhoto`: To-One → PhotoEntity (inverse: `favoritedBySouvenir`)
   108→- `shares`: To-Many → ShareEntity (inverse: `souvenir`)
   109→
   110→---
   111→
   112→### ShareEntity
   113→
   114→| Attribute                  | Type    | Optional | Default | Notes                           |
   115→| -------------------------- | ------- | -------- | ------- | ------------------------------- |
   116→| id                         | UUID    | No       | -       | PK                              |
   117→| token                      | String  | No       | -       | Legacy invite token             |
   118→| createdAt                  | Date    | No       | Date()  |                                 |
   119→| expiresAt                  | Date    | No       | -       |                                 |
   120→| isRevoked                  | Boolean | No       | false   |                                 |
   121→| inviterUserId              | String  | Yes      | -       | v2: iCloud record name of owner |
   122→| participantUserId          | String  | Yes      | -       | v2: iCloud record name of guest |
   123→| status                     | String  | Yes      | -       | v2: pending/active/declined/revoked |
   124→| acceptedAt                 | Date    | Yes      | -       | v2: when participant accepted   |
   125→| revokedAt                  | Date    | Yes      | -       | v2: when share was revoked      |
   126→| cloudKitShareRecordName    | String  | Yes      | -       | v2: CKShare record name         |
   127→| cloudKitRootRecordName     | String  | Yes      | -       | v2: shared CKRecord name        |
   128→| role                       | String  | Yes      | -       | v2: viewer/editor               |
   129→
   130→**Relationships:**
   131→
   132→- `souvenir`: To-One → SouvenirEntity (inverse: `shares`)
   133→
   134→---
   135→
   136→## Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD)
   137→
   138→```
   139→┌──────────────────┐
   140→│ ContactEntity    │
   141→├──────────────────┤
   142→│ • id (UUID) PK   │
   143→│ • compositeName  │
   144→│ • givenName      │
   145→│ • familyName     │
   146→│ • phoneE164      │
   147→│ • email          │
   148→│ • organization   │
   149→│ • address        │
   150→│ • notes          │
   151→│ • imageThumbData │
   152→│ • createdAt      │
   153→│ • externalId     │
   154→└──────────────────┘
   155→        ↑ primaryContact (1:1)
   156→        │
   157→        │ photos (M:N) ←──────────────┐
   158→        │                              │
   159→        ↓                              │
   160→┌──────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────┐
   161→│ SouvenirEntity   │          │ PhotoEntity      │
   162→├──────────────────┤          ├──────────────────┤
   163→│ • id (UUID) PK   │ photos   │ • id (UUID) PK   │
   164→│ • title          │ ←──(M:N)─┤ • assetLocalId   │
   165→│ • caption        │          │ • timestamp      │
   166→│ • createdAt      │ fav (1:1)│ • timezoneID     │
   167→│ • updatedAt      │ ←────────┤ • lat/long       │
   168→│ • lastLatitude   │          │ • postalGeoJSON  │
   169→│ • lastLongitude  │          │ • w3w            │
   170→│   (lat/long)     │                   │
   171→└──────────────────┘                   │
   172→        │                              └──→ contacts (M:N)
   173→        │ shares (1:M)
   174→        ↓
   175→┌──────────────────┐
   176→│ ShareEntity      │
   177→├──────────────────┤
   178→│ • id (UUID) PK   │
   179→│ • token          │
   180→│ • createdAt      │
   181→│ • expiresAt      │
   182→│ • isRevoked      │
   183→└──────────────────┘
   184→```
   185→
   186→### Relationship Details
   187→
   188→**ContactEntity ↔ SouvenirEntity:**
   189→- `ContactEntity.primarySouvenir` → `SouvenirEntity` (to-one, optional, inverse: `primaryContact`)
   190→- `SouvenirEntity.primaryContact` → `ContactEntity` (to-one, optional, inverse: `primarySouvenir`)
   191→
   192→**ContactEntity ↔ PhotoEntity:**
   193→- `ContactEntity.photos` → `Set<PhotoEntity>` (to-many, inverse: `contacts`)
   194→- `PhotoEntity.contacts` → `Set<ContactEntity>` (to-many, inverse: `photos`)
   195→
   196→**SouvenirEntity ↔ PhotoEntity:**
   197→- `SouvenirEntity.photos` → `Set<PhotoEntity>` (to-many, inverse: `souvenirs`)
   198→- `PhotoEntity.souvenirs` → `Set<SouvenirEntity>` (to-many, inverse: `photos`)
   199→- `SouvenirEntity.favoritePhoto` → `PhotoEntity` (to-one, optional, inverse: `favoritedBySouvenir`)
   200→- `PhotoEntity.favoritedBySouvenir` → `SouvenirEntity` (to-one, optional, inverse: `favoritePhoto`)
   201→
   202→**SouvenirEntity ↔ ShareEntity:**
   203→- `SouvenirEntity.shares` → `Set<ShareEntity>` (to-many, inverse: `souvenir`)
   204→- `ShareEntity.souvenir` → `SouvenirEntity` (to-one, inverse: `shares`)
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## Derived Properties and Computed Fields
   209→
   210→### SouvenirEntity Derivations
   211→
   212→**lastLatitude, lastLongitude:**
   213→- These are **stored** attributes (not Swift computed properties)
   214→- Updated automatically when photos are added/removed/modified
   215→- Represent the most recent photo's metadata for efficient querying
   216→- Update logic should be in:
   217→  - PhotoEntity add/remove operations
   218→  - Background sync tasks
   219→  - PhotoEntity metadata updates
   220→
   221→**displayTitle (Swift computed property):**
   222→```swift
   223→public var displayTitle: String {
   224→    if let t = title, !t.isEmpty { return t }
   225→    return primaryContact?.displayName ?? "Untitled Souvenir"
   226→}
   227→```
   228→
   229→### ContactEntity Derivations
   230→
   231→**displayName (Swift computed property):**
   232→```swift
   233→public var displayName: String {
   234→    if let composite = compositeName, !composite.isEmpty { return composite }
   235→    let parts = [givenName, familyName].compactMap { $0 }.filter { !$0.isEmpty }
   236→    if !parts.isEmpty { return parts.joined(separator: " ") }
   237→    if let org = organization, !org.isEmpty { return org }
   238→    if let phone = phoneE164, !phone.isEmpty { return phone }
   239→    return "Unknown Contact"
   240→}
   241→```
   242→
   243→---
   244→
   245→## Instructions for Xcode
   246→
   247→1. **Create the model:**
   248→   - File → New → File → Data Model
   249→   - Name: `SouvenirModel`
   250→   - Add four entities: `ContactEntity`, `PhotoEntity`, `SouvenirEntity`, `ShareEntity`
   251→
   252→2. **Configure each entity:**
   253→   - Set "Class" field to match entity name (ContactEntity, PhotoEntity, SouvenirEntity, ShareEntity)
   254→   - Set "Module" to "Current Product Module"
   255→   - Add attributes and relationships as specified above
   256→
   257→3. **Set Codegen:**
   258→   - For each entity, set "Codegen" to "Manual/None" (we've created custom extensions)
   259→
   260→4. **Indexing (Optional):**
   261→   - Core Data automatically optimizes queries based on usage patterns
   262→   - Manual indexing via "Fetch Index Elements" is rarely needed
   263→   - Ignore the "Indexed" column in the tables above—it's for documentation only
   264→
   265→---
   266→
   267→## Migration Strategy
   268→
   269→- **Version 1:** Initial schema (`SouvenirModel.xcdatamodel`)
   270→- **Version 2:** ShareEntity expanded for CloudKit sharing beta — 8 optional fields added (`SouvenirModel 2.xcdatamodel`, current)
   271→- **Future versions:** Use lightweight migrations (already enabled in PersistenceController)
   272→- For complex migrations, create mapping models manually
   273→
   274→### Adding New Attributes (Lightweight Migration)
   275→1. Open `SouvenirModel.xcdatamodeld`
   276→2. Editor → Add Model Version...
   277→3. Add new optional attribute with default value
   278→4. Set new version as current
   279→5. Update generated Swift files
   280→6. Test migration with existing data
   281→
   282→### Removing/Renaming Attributes (Heavyweight Migration)
   283→1. **HALT:** Consult team before proceeding
   284→2. Create mapping model
   285→3. Implement custom migration logic
   286→4. Extensive testing required
   287→5. Document in migration notes
   288→
   289→---
   290→
   291→## CloudKit (Deferred)
   292→
   293→CloudKit sync is deferred for now. When ready:
   294→- Add iCloud capability in target settings
   295→- Update `PersistenceController` per comments in that file
   296→- No schema changes needed!
   297→
   298→---
   299→## Data Integrity Rules
   300→
   301→### SouvenirEntity Integrity
   302→1. **Must have at least one photo** (enforced at business logic layer, not DB constraint)
   303→2. **Primary contact is optional** but recommended for display
   304→3. **Favorite photo must be in photos set** if present
   305→4. **lastLatitude/lastLongitude fields must stay synchronized** with photos set
   306→
   307→### PhotoEntity Integrity
   308→1. **assetLocalId must be unique** if present (PHAsset reference)
   309→2. **Timestamp required for sorting** and frecency calculations
   310→3. **Location data (lat/long) must be paired** or both nil
   311→4. **timezoneID format**: IANA timezone identifier (e.g., "America/Chicago")
   312→
   313→### ContactEntity Integrity
   314→1. **At least one identifier required**: compositeName, givenName, phoneE164, or email
   315→2. **phoneE164 format**: E.164 international format (e.g., "+12177217217")
   316→3. **externalIdentifier**: CNContact.identifier for syncing with Contacts.app
   317→4. **imageThumbData**: JPEG or PNG binary data, target size ≤ 100KB
   318→
   319→### ShareEntity Integrity
   320→1. **token must be unique** and cryptographically signed
   321→2. **expiresAt must be in the future** at creation time
   322→3. **souvenir relationship must reference existing SouvenirEntity**
   323→4. **Short-lived tokens recommended**: 24-48 hours max
   324→
   325→### Cascade Delete Rules
   326→**ContactEntity deletion:**
   327→- Nullify `SouvenirEntity.primaryContact`
   328→- Remove from `PhotoEntity.contacts` (many-to-many dissociation)
   329→
   330→**PhotoEntity deletion:**
   331→- Remove from `ContactEntity.photos`
   332→- Remove from `SouvenirEntity.photos`
   333→- Nullify `SouvenirEntity.favoritePhoto` if matched
   334→- **Trigger recomputation** of `SouvenirEntity.lastLatitude/lastLongitude` fields
   335→
   336→**SouvenirEntity deletion:**
   337→- Cascade delete behavior: **DECIDE POLICY**
   338→  - Option A: Keep orphaned photos (default)
   339→  - Option B: Cascade delete all photos
   340→  - Currently using Option A
   341→-- **Cascade delete all ShareEntity records** (shares are meaningless without souvenir)
   342→
   343→**ShareEntity deletion:**
   344→- No cascade effects (leaf entity)
   345→
   346→---
   347→
   348→## Performance Considerations
   349→
   350→### Fetch Request Optimization
   351→```swift
   352→// Efficient souvenir list fetch (sorted by recency)
   353→let request = SouvenirEntity.fetchRequest()
   354→request.sortDescriptors = [NSSortDescriptor(key: "updatedAt", ascending: false)]
   355→request.relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching = ["primaryContact", "favoritePhoto"]
   356→
   357→// Efficient photo fetch with location
   358→let photoRequest = PhotoEntity.fetchRequest()
   359→photoRequest.predicate = NSPredicate(format: "latitude != nil AND longitude != nil")
   360→photoRequest.sortDescriptors = [NSSortDescriptor(key: "timestamp", ascending: false)]
   361→
   362→// Active (non-expired, non-revoked) shares
   363→let shareRequest = ShareEntity.fetchRequest()
   364→shareRequest.predicate = NSPredicate(format: "expiresAt > %@ AND isRevoked == NO", Date() as NSDate)
   365→shareRequest.sortDescriptors = [NSSortDescriptor(key: "createdAt", ascending: false)]
   366→```
   367→
   368→### Indexing Strategy
   369→- **UUID primary keys**: Always indexed
   370→- **Foreign key lookups**: `externalIdentifier`, `assetLocalId`, `phoneE164`
   371→- **Sorting fields**: `timestamp`, `updatedAt`
   372→- **Family/given names**: Indexed for search
   373→
   374→### Batch Operations
   375→- Use `NSBatchInsertRequest` for importing large photo sets
   376→- Use `NSBatchUpdateRequest` for bulk metadata updates
   377→- Use `NSBatchDeleteRequest` with caution (bypasses validation)
   378→
   379→---
   380→
   381→## Testing and Validation
   382→
   383→### Unit Test Requirements
   384→```swift
   385→// Test souvenir creation
   386→func testCreateSouvenir() async throws {
   387→    let context = PersistenceController.preview.container.viewContext
   388→    let souvenir = SouvenirEntity(context: context)
   389→    souvenir.id = UUID()
   390→    souvenir.createdAt = Date()
   391→    souvenir.updatedAt = Date()
   392→    try context.save()
   393→    #expect(souvenir.id != nil)
   394→}
   395→
   396→// Test relationship integrity
   397→func testSouvenirPhotoRelationship() async throws {
   398→    // Create souvenir and photo, verify bidirectional relationship
   399→}
   400→
   401→// Test derived field updates
   402→func testLatestPhotoLocationUpdate() async throws {
   403→    // Add photos, verify lastLatitude/lastLongitude updates correctly
   404→}
   405→
   406→// Test share token lifecycle
   407→func testShareCreationAndExpiry() async throws {
   408→    let context = PersistenceController.preview.container.viewContext
   409→    let souvenir = SouvenirEntity(context: context)
   410→    souvenir.id = UUID()
   411→    
   412→    let share = ShareEntity(context: context)
   413→    share.id = UUID()
   414→    share.souvenir = souvenir
   415→    share.token = "test-token-123"
   416→    share.createdAt = Date()
   417→    share.expiresAt = Date().addingTimeInterval(86400) // 24 hours
   418→    share.souvenir = souvenir
   419→    
   420→    try context.save()
   421→    #expect(share.isRevoked == false)
   422→    #expect(share.souvenir?.id == souvenir.id)
   423→}
   424→```
   425→
   426→### Manual Verification Steps
   427→1. Open `SouvenirModel.xcdatamodeld` in Xcode
   428→2. Select each entity, verify:
   429→   - Class name matches entity name
   430→   - Module set to "Current Product Module"
   431→   - Codegen set to "Manual/None"
   432→3. Verify all attributes present with correct types
   433→4. Verify all relationships with correct cardinality and inverse
   434→5. Build project, ensure `*+CoreDataProperties.swift` files compile
   435→6. Run app, create test data, verify persistence
   436→

1→---
     2→title: feat: Phone Number Discovery (Lightweight MVP)
     3→type: feat
     4→date: 2026-02-23
     5→---
     6→
     7→# feat: Phone Number Discovery (Lightweight MVP)
     8→
     9→## Overview
    10→Enable users to find each other by phone number with a lightweight, low‑touch flow: opt‑in phone verification via SMS, a minimal directory lookup service, and in‑app results that drive existing share‑link invites.
    11→
    12→## Problem Statement / Motivation
    13→Today, sharing is link‑only (iMessage). Users want to discover friends by phone number without full account creation. We need a low‑friction, privacy‑conscious way to verify ownership of a phone number and resolve matches for opt‑in discovery.
    14→
    15→## Proposed Solution
    16→Lightweight phone verification + directory lookup service (minimal backend):
    17→- Client normalizes phone to E.164 and performs SMS verification (one‑time code).
    18→- Backend stores a **salted hash of E.164** and a **stable app user ID** (CloudKit record name or app‑generated UUID), plus **opt‑in discoverability flag**.
    19→- Lookup: client submits hashed phone(s) (from contact selection) → backend returns matched user IDs + minimal profile metadata and/or a short‑lived invite token/URL.
    20→- Use existing share‑link flow for invites; no full account system yet.
    21→
    22→## Technical Considerations
    23→- **Privacy & security:**
    24→  - Store only salted hashes of phone numbers, not raw numbers.
    25→  - Require explicit opt‑in to be discoverable by phone.
    26→  - Rate limit lookups and verification attempts.
    27→  - Avoid contact list upload by default; use user‑selected numbers or on‑device hashing.
    28→- **Identity:**
    29→  - Use iCloud user record name or app‑generated ID as the directory key.
    30→  - Store device‑local “verified phone” state in Core Data or UserDefaults.
    31→- **Backend footprint (MVP):**
    32→  - 2–3 endpoints: `start_verification`, `verify_code`, `lookup`.
    33→  - Provider‑managed SMS (Twilio Verify or equivalent) to avoid building telecom logic.
    34→- **CloudKit usage:**
    35→  - CloudKit user identity discovery by phone/email is deprecated; don’t depend on it for discovery.
    36→  - CloudKit can still store app state after verification, but lookup needs a server for abuse control.
    37→- **Data model:**
    38→  - Avoid Core Data schema changes if possible; store local flags only.
    39→  - If a new Core Data entity is required, add additively and update `docs/schema.md` with migration plan.
    40→
    41→## SpecFlow Analysis (Gaps & Flows)
    42→### User Flow Overview
    43→1. Opt‑in discovery → enter phone → receive SMS → enter code → verified.
    44→2. Find by phone → select number(s) → lookup → show match card(s) → send share link.
    45→3. Opt‑out → disable discoverability → directory entry disabled.
    46→
    47→### Missing Elements & Gaps
    48→- **Error handling:** retry rules for SMS failures; “no match” UX; expired codes.
    49→- **Abuse controls:** lookup rate limits, verification throttling, and audit logging.
    50→- **Privacy:** explicit copy for opt‑in/out; handling of contact access vs manual entry.
    51→- **Recovery:** what happens on reinstall or device change without full auth.
    52→
    53→### Critical Questions
    54→1. Should discovery require full Contacts permission, or manual phone entry only for MVP?
    55→2. What minimal profile data can be returned on match (name? avatar? none)?
    56→3. How long should verification state remain valid without re‑verification?
    57→
    58→## Acceptance Criteria
    59→- [ ] Users can verify phone via SMS and opt in to “discoverable by phone.”
    60→- [ ] Lookups return matches only for opt‑in users.
    61→- [ ] Lookup response contains a stable user ID and safe minimal metadata.
    62→- [ ] Rate limiting and verification throttling are enforced.
    63→- [ ] Users can opt out and remove directory presence.
    64→- [ ] Tests cover phone normalization, verification state, and lookup handling.
    65→
    66→## Success Metrics
    67→- Verification completion rate.
    68→- Median time from “Find by phone” to “invite sent.”
    69→- Lookup error rate and rate‑limit hits.
    70→
    71→## Dependencies & Risks
    72→- SMS provider availability/costs and compliance.
    73→- Privacy policy updates for phone verification and discovery.
    74→- Risk of enumeration if lookup isn’t protected.
    75→- Without full auth, identity recovery on reinstall is limited.
    76→
    77→## ERD (Server‑Side MVP)
    78→```mermaid
    79→erDiagram
    80→  USER_DIRECTORY {
    81→    string user_id
    82→    string phone_hash
    83→    boolean discoverable
    84→    datetime verified_at
    85→    datetime updated_at
    86→  }
    87→  VERIFICATION_SESSION {
    88→    string session_id
    89→    string phone_hash
    90→    datetime expires_at
    91→    int attempts
    92→    string status
    93→  }
    94→
    95→  USER_DIRECTORY ||--o{ VERIFICATION_SESSION : "verifies"
    96→```
    97→
    98→## References & Research
    99→- docs/technical-notes.md
   100→- docs/cloudkit-sharing.md
   101→- docs/schema.md
   102→- Apple: CKDiscoverUserIdentitiesOperation (deprecated)
   103→  - https://developer.apple.com/documentation/cloudkit/ckdiscoveruseridentitiesoperation
   104→- Twilio Verify best practices
   105→  - https://www.twilio.com/docs/verify/developer-best-practices
   106→- Twilio Verify rate limits
   107→  - https://www.twilio.com/docs/verify/api/programmable-rate-limits
   108→

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→---
     2→title: feat: DevTools Dual Invite Links for Shared Souvenir
     3→type: feat
     4→date: 2026-02-23
     5→---
     6→
     7→# feat: DevTools Dual Invite Links for Shared Souvenir
     8→
     9→## Overview
    10→Add a DevTools-only flow to select a device contact (system contacts), choose a photo, and generate two distinct invite links that both point to the same CloudKit share for a newly created souvenir. This enables an admin to introduce two people without any backend or discovery.
    11→
    12→## Problem Statement / Motivation
    13→We want a lightweight admin tool to seed a shared souvenir between two people without backend discovery. The app already supports CloudKit share links; DevTools should streamline creating a share and distributing two per-recipient links.
    14→
    15→## Proposed Solution
    16→Create a DevTools section that:
    17→- Lets the admin pick two **system contacts** (CNContactStore).
    18→- Lets the admin pick a **photo** (PHPhotoLibrary).
    19→- Creates a new Souvenir + Photo locally, sets the primary contact to the “host” contact (first selected), then creates a **CKShare** for the souvenir.
    20→- Generates two **distinct wrapper links** (unique invite tokens) that **both resolve to the same CKShare URL**.
    21→- Presents per-contact Share buttons (Message UI or UIActivityViewController) with the link prefilled.
    22→
    23→### Key Constraints (No Backend)
    24→- CloudKit supports **one CKShare per root record**; there is only one true share URL.
    25→- Without a backend, “distinct links” can only be **wrapper URLs** (different tokens) that both open the same CKShare URL in the app.
    26→- No “is on SVNR” discovery without a server; this is admin-only, link-based.
    27→
    28→## Technical Considerations
    29→- **Contacts:** Use `CNContactPickerViewController` (system contacts only), request Contacts permission.
    30→- **Photos:** Use `PHPickerViewController` or existing photo selection flow; store assetLocalId on PhotoEntity.
    31→- **Core Data rules:** One souvenir per primary contact; keep primaryContact set (host contact). Ensure `phoneE164` normalization for contacts if importing.
    32→- **Share creation:** Use existing CloudKit share flow (souvenir as CKShare root).
    33→- **Invite links:**
    34→  - Wrapper link format (example): `souvenir://share?ckshare=<encoded_ckshare_url>&invite=<uuid>`
    35→  - App handles wrapper → opens CKShare URL.
    36→  - Tokens are for analytics/manual tracking only (no server validation).
    37→- **DevTools gating:** DEBUG-only UI.
    38→- **Privacy:** Admin-only feature; no contact upload or discovery.
    39→
    40→## User Flow Overview
    41→1. Open DevTools → “Admin: Dual Invite.”
    42→2. Pick Contact A (host), Contact B (guest) from system contacts.
    43→3. Pick a photo.
    44→4. Generate CKShare + two wrapper links (Invite A, Invite B).
    45→5. Tap Share for each link and send to the selected contact.
    46→
    47→## Acceptance Criteria
    48→- [ ] DevTools shows a “Dual Invite” section (DEBUG only).
    49→- [ ] Admin can select two **system contacts** and one photo.
    50→- [ ] App creates a new Souvenir + Photo and generates a CKShare URL.
    51→- [ ] Two **distinct wrapper links** are generated and displayed.
    52→- [ ] Each link can be shared via message/share sheet.
    53→- [ ] App can open the wrapper link and resolve to the CKShare URL.
    54→
    55→## Testing
    56→- [ ] Unit test: wrapper link encode/decode and CKShare URL extraction.
    57→- [ ] Unit test: contact import/normalization for selected CNContact.
    58→- [ ] Manual: end‑to‑end flow (create link, accept on second device).
    59→
    60→## Dependencies & Risks
    61→- Contacts permission flow for admin device.
    62→- Wrapper links are not enforceably unique; they are cosmetic without backend.
    63→- Contact schema: ensure primaryContact is set to avoid validation errors.
    64→
    65→## References & Research
    66→- docs/technical-notes.md
    67→- docs/cloudkit-sharing.md
    68→- docs/schema.md
    69→

1→# SVNR Roadmap — Trust Forest MVP
     2→
     3→**Last updated:** 2026-02-18  
     4→**Current focus:** Ship a usable Trust Forest MVP (not full internet-scale P2P yet) and a narrow beta social feature: Shared Souvenir (2-party)
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## North Star
     9→
    10→SVNR helps us share the right souvenir with the right people at the right depth of context.
    11→
    12→Trust Forest is the social engine:
    13→- rings (5 / 15 / 50 / 150 / 500)
    14→- vouching as trust propagation
    15→- souvenirs as living vouchers of trust anchors
    16→
    17→This roadmap narrows scope to a shippable MVP.
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## What We Are Shipping in MVP
    22→
    23→### 1) Ring model in product (core)
    24→- [ ] Ring taxonomy implemented in app model:
    25→  - Ring 1 (5) intimate
    26→  - Ring 2 (15) close
    27→  - Ring 3 (50) friends
    28→  - Ring 4 (150) community
    29→  - Ring 5 (500) acquaintances
    30→- [ ] Per-contact ring assignment UI (manual first)
    31→- [ ] Ring-aware share targeting in composer
    32→- [ ] Ring legend/explainer in onboarding/settings
    33→
    34→**Acceptance:** User can assign contacts to rings and choose share-by-ring.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→### 2) Vouching MVP (manual + explicit)
    39→- [ ] "Vouch for this person" action in contact/profile UI
    40→- [ ] Store vouch edges with source/target/timestamp/confidence
    41→- [ ] Vouch feed in trust detail view (who vouched for whom)
    42→- [ ] Basic vouch influence rule (no heavy automation yet):
    43→  - stronger effect from nearer rings
    44→  - influence decays with distance
    45→
    46→**Acceptance:** User can create and inspect vouches; graph reflects raised baseline hints.
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→### 3) Souvenirs as living vouchers
    51→- [ ] Mark a souvenir as "trust anchor" (optional flag)
    52→- [ ] Display anchor history on relationship timeline
    53→- [ ] Relationship page shows:
    54→  - shared anchors count
    55→  - latest anchor
    56→  - vouch-related context
    57→- [ ] Sharing flow supports contextual depth presets by ring
    58→
    59→**Acceptance:** Shared moments visibly influence relationship context in-app.
    60→
    61→---
    62→
    63→## Beta Focus: Shared Souvenir (2-party)
    64→
    65→This beta feature is intentionally narrow and supports Trust Forest MVP by making a souvenir a living, shared object between two people.
    66→
    67→### Goals
    68→- A single Souvenir can be shared between exactly two parties.
    69→- Either party can add photos; both see updates.
    70→- Sharing is private and not re-shareable.
    71→
    72→### Sharing Paths (MVP)
    73→- **Link share (first path):**
    74→  - Share link includes a one-time token delivered via iMessage to a known contact.
    75→  - Recipient must install the app (TestFlight alpha required) and explicitly approve the share.
    76→- **In-app share (second path, more prominent):**
    77→  - Search by imported contact, invite them to a shared souvenir.
    78→  - Requires user lookup via contact import + app identity mapping.
    79→
    80→### UX Placement
    81→- The shared experience lives inside the existing `SouvenirView`.
    82→  - Show shared state, participant, and shared photo feed context in the same view.
    83→
    84→### Sync + Storage
    85→- CloudKit-backed sync for shared souvenirs and shared photo additions.
    86→- No reshare or third-party access in beta.
    87→
    88→### Acceptance
    89→- Two-party share created and accepted.
    90→- Both parties can add photos and see updates within the same shared souvenir.
    91→- Recipient can decline; share is revoked cleanly if needed.
    92→
    93→---
    94→
    95→### 4) Trust Forest visualization (inside app + design artifacts)
    96→- [ ] In-app Trust Forest view (v1):
    97→  - concentric ring layout around self
    98→  - contact nodes by ring
    99→  - optional vouch links overlay
   100→- [ ] Miro boards as product/design spec artifacts:
   101→  - Rings
   102→  - Vouch Flow
   103→  - Souvenir Lifecycle
   104→- [ ] Visual style aligns with Trust Forest spec language
   105→
   106→**Acceptance:** Team can demo ring/vouch/souvenir model visually and interactively.
   107→
   108→---
   109→
   110→### 5) Safety, privacy, and defaults
   111→- [ ] Private-by-default sharing
   112→- [ ] Explicit consent language around vouching visibility
   113→- [ ] No opaque trust score shown to users
   114→- [ ] Explainability for why someone was suggested as relevant
   115→
   116→**Acceptance:** Sharing and vouch outcomes are inspectable and reversible.
   117→
   118→---
   119→
   120→## Explicitly Out of Scope for MVP
   121→
   122→- Full internet-wide autonomous trust propagation
   123→- Complex auto-ring reassignment
   124→- Enterprise/org trust overlays
   125→- Multi-hop algorithmic ranking at scale
   126→- Heavy video generation pipeline inside product
   127→
   128→These can follow once MVP behavior is validated with real users.
   129→
   130→---
   131→
   132→## MVP Milestones
   133→
   134→## Milestone A — Data + UX foundation (Week 1-2)
   135→- Ring schema in Core Data
   136→- Ring assignment UI
   137→- Share-by-ring composer
   138→
   139→## Milestone B — Vouching + anchors (Week 3-4)
   140→- Manual vouch action + storage
   141→- Trust anchor flag + relationship timeline
   142→- Basic trust detail screens
   143→
   144→## Milestone C — Trust Forest view + polish (Week 5-6)
   145→- Forest visualization v1
   146→- Explainability/safety polish
   147→- Internal dogfood with close testers
   148→
   149→## Milestone D — MVP release candidate (Week 7-8)
   150→- QA pass and bug triage
   151→- Copy/UX refinements
   152→- Beta release to controlled cohort
   153→
   154→---
   155→
   156→## Success Criteria (MVP)
   157→
   158→- Users can reliably choose *who needs to know* via rings
   159→- Users understand and use vouching without confusion
   160→- Souvenirs strengthen relationship context (not just media storage)
   161→- Team can point to measurable reduction in over-sharing/under-sharing friction
   162→
   163→---
   164→
   165→## Canonical Docs (SVNR)
   166→
   167→- `docs/trust-graph-forest-weave.md` — narrative + product philosophy spec
   168→- `docs/paid-feature-evaluation.md` — technical mechanics and implementation notes
   169→- `docs/forests-naming-research.md` — candidate implementation names
   170→- `docs/archive/roadmap-pre-trust-forest-2026-02-18.md` — previous roadmap snapshot
   171→

content.content:
1→# CONSTITUTION.md - Core Values & Response Protocol
     2→
     3→## Core Values
     4→
     5→1. **Honesty over progress.** Say what's true, not what sounds good.
     6→2. **Clarity before cleverness.** Simple beats smart when both work.
     7→3. **First principles before concluding.** Understand the foundation.
     8→4. **Compression without distortion.** Dense, not diluted.
     9→5. **Exploration without steering.** Follow curiosity, don't force direction.
    10→
    11→## Response Protocol
    12→
    13→**What to do:**
    14→- State premises plainly when reasoning (e.g., "Given that…", "In light of…")
    15→- Use the shortest answer that preserves meaning
    16→- Write working code that compiles
    17→- Use poetic language when it increases precision or compression
    18→- Trace to etymology when it sharpens understanding
    19→- Expand noncolloquial acronyms (TLAs, NGOs, jargon alphabet-speak)
    20→
    21→**What to skip:**
    22→- Follow-up questions not germane to the task
    23→- Nudges, advice, steering, selling (however subtle)
    24→- Warnings, hedges, disclaimers, moralizing, paternalism
    25→- Padding for politeness or completeness
    26→- Conversational fillers
    27→- Meta-commentary
    28→- Unrequested elaboration or documentation
    29→- Closing remarks
    30→
    31→**Style:**
    32→Say the thing. Stop.
    33→
    34→**Exception:**
    35→If tone lacks enjoyment, guide back to bliss. Work should feel good.
    36→
    37→---
    38→
    39→This document governs how we communicate. For who we are and how we operate together, see `~/house/keep/AGENTS.md`.
    40→

content.content:
1→# The Trust Graph: Codifying Human Connection
     2→
     3→**A design pattern for social systems that mirrors how trust actually works in human relationships**
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## The Problem
     8→
     9→Modern social networks treat relationships as binary: you're either connected or you're not. Friend or stranger. Follower or blocked. Public or private.
    10→
    11→But human trust doesn't work that way.
    12→
    13→You don't share the same things with your best friend, your college roommate, your coworker, and your distant cousin. You calibrate what you share based on intimacy, context, and history. You trust some people with your deepest secrets, others with weekend plans, and still others with nothing more than a friendly wave.
    14→
    15→**The question:** Can we build systems that understand trust the way humans actually experience it?
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## The Solution: The Trust Graph
    20→
    21→The trust graph is a **dynamic, transitive network of relationships** where:
    22→
    23→1. **Trust has depth** — connections aren't binary (friend/stranger) but graduated along multiple dimensions
    24→2. **Trust flows through relationships** — if A trusts B and B trusts C, that creates a weak transitive link between A and C
    25→3. **Trust has natural limits** — inspired by Dunbar's numbers, intimacy tiers prevent cognitive overload
    26→4. **Trust suggests behavior** — the system guides what to share, not what to hide
    27→
    28→Think of it as **relationship DNA encoded in a graph structure**.
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## Core Principles
    33→
    34→### 1. Graduated Intimacy (Not Binary Connection)
    35→
    36→Instead of "friend or not," relationships exist on a spectrum:
    37→
    38→| Tier | Dunbar Limit | Label | Typical Sharing |
    39→|------|--------------|-------|-----------------|
    40→| **1** | 5 | Besties | Everything. Raw vulnerability. Current location, unfiltered thoughts, late-night confessions. |
    41→| **2** | 15 | Close Friends | Most things. City-level location, day-precise timestamps, context-rich stories. |
    42→| **3** | 50 | Friends | Curated sharing. Week-level timestamps, general location, public-appropriate content. |
    43→| **4** | 150 | Acquaintances | Public-equivalent. Month/country metadata, polished content, minimal context. |
    44→
    45→*Note: Numbers can be tuned per context, but the principle remains: trust depth correlates with sharing depth.*
    46→
    47→### 2. Transitive Trust (Trust Flows Through Networks)
    48→
    49→If **Alice** trusts **Bob** at Tier 1, and **Bob** trusts **Carol** at Tier 2, then:
    50→- Alice has a **Tier 3 transitive connection** to Carol (trust decays by one tier per hop)
    51→- If Bob suggests Alice connect directly to Carol, that suggestion carries weight (trusted introduction)
    52→- Alice can see Carol exists in Bob's network without Carol seeing Alice (asymmetric visibility until mutual trust forms)
    53→
    54→**Why this matters:**
    55→- **Warm introductions** replace cold connections
    56→- **Shared context** (mutual friend) provides social lubrication
    57→- **Spam resistance** built into network topology (can't DM tier-1 connections of strangers)
    58→
    59→**Decay rules:**
    60→- Each hop reduces trust tier by 1 (Tier 1 → Tier 2 → Tier 3 → Tier 4 → stranger)
    61→- At 3 hops, transitive trust expires (prevents six-degrees-of-Kevin-Bacon spam)
    62→- Users can block transitive trust propagation for specific relationships (privacy firewall)
    63→
    64→### 3. Dunbar-Number Guardrails (Organic Cognitive Limits)
    65→
    66→**Why Dunbar matters:**
    67→
    68→Anthropologist Robin Dunbar discovered humans can maintain:
    69→- ~5 intimate bonds (besties who know your soul)
    70→- ~15 close friendships (people you'd invite to dinner)
    71→- ~50 good friends (people you'd call in a crisis)
    72→- ~150 meaningful acquaintances (people you recognize and care about)
    73→
    74→**What the system does:**
    75→
    76→- **Soft warnings** when approaching tier limits: "You have 4/5 Tier 1 connections. Adding a 5th?"
    77→- **Tier promotion suggestions:** "You've messaged Jamie 47 times this month. Promote to Tier 2?"
    78→- **Automatic decay:** If you haven't interacted with someone in 6 months, suggest tier demotion (trust requires maintenance)
    79→
    80→**Why not enforce hard limits?**
    81→
    82→Some people maintain 7 besties, some maintain 3. The numbers are *guidelines*, not laws. The system suggests, doesn't dictate.
    83→
    84→### 4. Trust Suggests, Doesn't Restrict
    85→
    86→Traditional social media asks: **"What should I hide?"**
    87→
    88→The trust graph asks: **"What makes sense to share at this tier?"**
    89→
    90→**Examples:**
    91→
    92→- **Tier 1 share:** Full-resolution photo with GPS coordinates, raw caption ("Crying in the Starbucks bathroom because I miss my ex")
    93→- **Tier 2 share:** Photo with city-level location, edited caption ("Tough day but we got this")
    94→- **Tier 3 share:** Photo with no location, generic caption ("Coffee break vibes")
    95→- **Tier 4 share:** Photo, no metadata, no caption ("☕")
    96→
    97→**The system doesn't prevent** you from sharing tier-1 content with tier-4 connections. It just asks: *"This includes precise GPS data. Share with all 137 acquaintances?"*
    98→
    99→---
   100→
   101→## How Transitive Trust Works (Technical)
   102→
   103→### The Graph Structure
   104→
   105→```
   106→Node: Person
   107→Edges: Directed trust relationships with metadata
   108→
   109→Alice --[tier=1, verified=true, since=2023]-> Bob
   110→Bob --[tier=2, verified=true, since=2024]-> Carol
   111→Alice --[tier=3, transitive=true, via=Bob]-> Carol
   112→```
   113→
   114→### Trust Path Calculation
   115→
   116→When Alice wants to share something with Carol:
   117→
   118→1. **Direct path?** Check if Alice → Carol edge exists (if so, use that tier)
   119→2. **Transitive path?** Find shortest path Alice → X → Y → Carol
   120→3. **Decay applied:** Start with strongest direct link tier, decay by 1 per hop
   121→4. **Max 3 hops:** Beyond 3 hops, treat as stranger (prevents spam via Kevin Bacon effect)
   122→
   123→**Example:**
   124→
   125→```
   126→Alice --[T1]-> Bob --[T2]-> Carol --[T1]-> Dave
   127→
   128→Alice's effective trust tier to Dave:
   129→- Via Bob: T1 → T2 (decay 1) → T3 (decay 1) = T3
   130→- Transitive connection exists: Dave appears in Alice's "Friends of Friends" with T3 effective tier
   131→```
   132→
   133→### Verification & Trust Anchoring
   134→
   135→**Cold start problem:** How do you trust the first person?
   136→
   137→**Solution:** In-person verification (like Signal's safety numbers)
   138→
   139→1. **Generate fingerprint** from user's public key (ed25519)
   140→2. **QR code exchange** when meeting IRL
   141→3. **Mutual scan** → both parties verify fingerprints match
   142→4. **Trust anchor established** → now transitive trust can flow through this verified edge
   143→
   144→**Why this works:**
   145→- IRL meeting = high trust baseline
   146→- Cryptographic verification prevents MITM attacks
   147→- Verified edges are marked in UI (checkmark badge)
   148→- Unverified edges show warning ("You haven't verified this connection")
   149→
   150→---
   151→
   152→## Applications
   153→
   154→### Social Networks (Private Sharing)
   155→
   156→**Problem:** Instagram shows your beach vacation to 847 followers, including your boss, your ex, and your dentist.
   157→
   158→**Trust Graph Solution:**
   159→- Share full-res photos + location metadata with Tier 1 (5 besties)
   160→- Share curated version (no location, flattering filter) with Tier 2 (15 close friends)
   161→- Share public-safe version (no metadata) with Tier 3+ (acquaintances)
   162→
   163→**User experience:** One post, tiered visibility automatically applied. No manual selection of "who can see this."
   164→
   165→### Messaging (Spam Resistance)
   166→
   167→**Problem:** DMs are either wide open (spam hell) or locked down (miss real connections).
   168→
   169→**Trust Graph Solution:**
   170→- **Tier 1-2:** Direct DMs always land in primary inbox
   171→- **Tier 3:** DMs land in "Friends" folder (checked less often)
   172→- **Tier 4 transitive:** DMs land in "Requests" folder with context ("Via Bob")
   173→- **No transitive path:** DMs blocked or require paid fee (spam tax)
   174→
   175→**Why this works:**
   176→- Warm introductions ("Bob's friend Carol") get through
   177→- Cold spam can't reach you (no path = no access)
   178→- You control tier assignments, so trusted connections always reach you
   179→
   180→### Professional Networks (Context-Aware Sharing)
   181→
   182→**Problem:** LinkedIn shows your weekend rock-climbing photos to recruiters and your side-project tweets to your current employer.
   183→
   184→**Trust Graph Solution:**
   185→
   186→Two orthogonal graphs:
   187→- **Personal trust graph** (intimacy-based)
   188→- **Professional trust graph** (context-based: colleague, mentor, client, recruiter)
   189→
   190→**Cross-graph rules:**
   191→- Personal Tier 1 + Professional "Colleague" = share weekend plans
   192→- Personal Tier 3 + Professional "Recruiter" = share only public resume content
   193→- Personal Tier 1 + Professional "None" = full personal sharing, no professional bleed
   194→
   195→### Collaborative Filtering (Discovery)
   196→
   197→**Problem:** Algorithmic recommendations show you content based on "engagement" (clickbait wins).
   198→
   199→**Trust Graph Solution:**
   200→
   201→- **Tier 1 shares** = auto-recommend to you (your besties have great taste)
   202→- **Tier 2 shares** = show with "15 friends liked this" context
   203→- **Tier 3+ shares** = only show if >30% of your Tier 1-2 network engaged
   204→
   205→**Why this works:**
   206→- Trust-based discovery > engagement-based discovery
   207→- Content quality signal = "people I trust love this"
   208→- Spam can't buy its way into your feed (no transitive path = not visible)
   209→
   210→---
   211→
   212→## Implementation Patterns
   213→
   214→### Data Model (Core Data / SQL)
   215→
   216→```swift
   217→// Node
   218→Person {
   219→    id: UUID
   220→    publicKey: Data (ed25519, 32 bytes)
   221→    fingerprint: String (base58, for human verification)
   222→}
   223→
   224→// Edge
   225→TrustRelationship {
   226→    from: Person
   227→    to: Person
   228→    tier: Int (1-4)
   229→    verified: Bool (true if in-person verification done)
   230→    since: Date
   231→    lastInteraction: Date
   232→    transitive: Bool (false for direct, true for computed)
   233→    via: Person? (if transitive, who's the intermediary?)
   234→}
   235→
   236→// Share
   237→Content {
   238→    id: UUID
   239→    creator: Person
   240→    data: Data (encrypted)
   241→    metadata: JSON (location, timestamp, etc.)
   242→    sharedWith: [TrustTier: MetadataPolicy]
   243→}
   244→
   245→MetadataPolicy {
   246→    tier: Int
   247→    includeGPS: Bool
   248→    locationPrecision: String (precise, city, country, none)
   249→    timestampPrecision: String (exact, day, week, month)
   250→}
   251→```
   252→
   253→### Algorithms
   254→
   255→**Trust Path Finder (BFS with decay):**
   256→
   257→```swift
   258→func effectiveTier(from: Person, to: Person, maxHops: Int = 3) -> Int? {
   259→    var queue: [(person: Person, tier: Int, hops: Int)] = [(from, 0, 0)]
   260→    var visited: Set<UUID> = []
   261→    var bestTier: Int? = nil
   262→
   263→    while let (current, currentTier, hops) = queue.popFirst() {
   264→        if current == to {
   265→            bestTier = max(bestTier ?? 5, currentTier) // lower number = higher trust
   266→            continue
   267→        }
   268→        
   269→        if hops >= maxHops { continue }
   270→        if visited.contains(current.id) { continue }
   271→        visited.insert(current.id)
   272→
   273→        for edge in current.outgoingEdges where edge.verified {
   274→            let decayedTier = edge.tier + 1 // trust decays by 1 per hop
   275→            queue.append((edge.to, decayedTier, hops + 1))
   276→        }
   277→    }
   278→
   279→    return bestTier
   280→}
   281→```
   282→
   283→**Tier Promotion Suggestion (interaction frequency):**
   284→
   285→```swift
   286→func suggestTierChanges(for user: Person) -> [TierSuggestion] {
   287→    var suggestions: [TierSuggestion] = []
   288→
   289→    for relationship in user.relationships {
   290→        let interactions = countInteractions(relationship, last: .months(3))
   291→        let currentTier = relationship.tier
   292→        
   293→        // Promotion rules
   294→        if currentTier == 2 && interactions > 40 {
   295→            suggestions.append(.promote(relationship, to: 1, reason: "You've messaged \(relationship.to.name) 40+ times this quarter"))
   296→        }
   297→        
   298→        // Demotion rules
   299→        if currentTier == 1 && daysSince(relationship.lastInteraction) > 180 {
   300→            suggestions.append(.demote(relationship, to: 2, reason: "You haven't connected with \(relationship.to.name) in 6 months"))
   301→        }
   302→    }
   303→
   304→    return suggestions
   305→}
   306→```
   307→
   308→---
   309→
   310→## Privacy & Security
   311→
   312→### What Users Control
   313→
   314→- **Tier assignment** (manual promotion/demotion at any time)
   315→- **Verification** (which connections have verified fingerprints)
   316→- **Transitive visibility toggle** (can disable "friends of friends" discovery per relationship)
   317→- **Data retention** (can set auto-delete timers per tier: T4 shares deleted after 30 days)
   318→
   319→### What the System Protects
   320→
   321→- **End-to-end encryption** for all content (only sender and tier-appropriate recipients decrypt)
   322→- **Metadata policies enforced** before share leaves device (GPS stripped for Tier 3+ before upload)
   323→- **No server-side trust graph** (graph stored locally, only anonymized edge counts synced for anti-spam)
   324→- **Transitive trust computed client-side** (server never knows who trusts whom)
   325→
   326→### Attack Resistance
   327→
   328→| Attack | Mitigation |
   329→|--------|------------|
   330→| **Spam via fake transitive paths** | Max 3 hops, verified edges only, no path = no DM |
   331→| **Social graph mining** | Server sees encrypted edges, not plaintext relationships |
   332→| **Malicious tier escalation** | Tier changes require mutual verification for T1-2 |
   333→| **Metadata leakage** | Metadata stripped client-side per tier policy, logged in audit trail |
   334→
   335→---
   336→
   337→## Design Philosophy
   338→
   339→### Inspired By Human Biology
   340→
   341→Dunbar's numbers aren't arbitrary—they emerge from **neocortex size**. Our brains physically constrain how many deep relationships we can maintain.
   342→
   343→The trust graph respects that constraint. It doesn't try to make you have 10,000 friends. It helps you maintain the 5-150 connections you already have, with appropriate depth.
   344→
   345→### Trust as a Living Structure
   346→
   347→Relationships aren't static. They grow, decay, change context. The trust graph is **dynamic**:
   348→
   349→- Interaction frequency suggests promotions
   350→- Lack of contact suggests demotions
   351→- New mutual friends create transitive links
   352→- Blocking someone severs their transitive access
   353→
   354→This mirrors how real friendship works: effort sustains trust, neglect erodes it.
   355→
   356→### Privacy by Design
   357→
   358→Traditional social networks ask: *"Who should be allowed to see this?"*
   359→
   360→The trust graph asks: *"What version makes sense for each tier?"*
   361→
   362→This flips the model from **restriction** (hide things) to **calibration** (share the right thing with the right people).
   363→
   364→---
   365→
   366→## Open Questions
   367→
   368→1. **Cross-platform trust?** If Alice trusts Bob on Platform A, should that trust carry to Platform B?
   369→   - *Proposal:* Export/import trust graph with user consent (signed data structure)
   370→
   371→2. **Organizational trust?** How do companies/communities use tiered trust?
   372→   - *Proposal:* Org-scoped trust graph (employees inherit transitive trust from org, but personal tiers override)
   373→
   374→3. **Trust decay rate?** How fast should unused relationships demote?
   375→   - *Proposal:* User-configurable (default: 6 months inactivity → suggest demotion)
   376→
   377→4. **AI agents as nodes?** Can an AI hold a tier-1 relationship?
   378→   - *Proposal:* Yes, if verified (cryptographic identity + user consent)
   379→
   380→---
   381→
   382→## Further Reading
   383→
   384→- **Dunbar's Number:** ["Neocortex size as a constraint on group size in primates"](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/004724849290081J) (Dunbar, 1992)
   385→- **Transitive Trust Models:** ["A Computational Model of Trust and Reputation"](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/3-540-36609-1_6) (Josang et al., 2003)
   386→- **Privacy-Preserving Social Graphs:** ["Improving User Privacy in Social Networks with Relationship-Based Access Control"](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/5487490) (Carminati et al., 2009)
   387→- **Signal's Trust Model:** [Signal Safety Numbers](https://signal.org/docs/specifications/sesame/) (Open Whisper Systems)
   388→
   389→---
   390→
   391→## License & Attribution
   392→
   393→This document is released under **CC BY-SA 4.0** (Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike).
   394→
   395→**Inspiration:**
   396→- Robin Dunbar (Dunbar's numbers)
   397→- Signal (cryptographic verification UX)
   398→- Paul Baran (distributed networks, 1964)
   399→- Worfeus & Chef (SVNR P2P investigation, 2026-02-17)
   400→
   401→**Source:** [openclaw/keep/docs/trust-graph.md](https://github.com/krry/openclaw/keep/docs/trust-graph.md)
   402→
   403→---
   404→
   405→**Status:** Living document (v1.0, 2026-02-18)
   406→
   407→**Feedback:** DM @worfeus on AICQ or email orfx@agentmail.to
   408→
